pattern: Every wrong-but-arithmetically-consistent answer corresponds to some other way of grouping the same sequence of operators into a binary tree, so recursively combining the sets of possible values of every left/right split of the expression (pruned to the range that could ever matter) enumerates exactly the answers a confused-but-honest student could reach.
hints: First compute the single correct answer by applying the standard rule: all multiplications left to right, then all additions left to right.

Any other order of operations a student might have used corresponds to picking some operator to apply last, splitting the expression into a left part and a right part evaluated independently first -- so the set of all reachable values for a range of the expression is built from combining every possible value of every left/right split at every operator position.

Since every valid test answer is capped at 1000, any intermediate combination bigger than that can never matter and can be safely dropped from the tracked set, keeping the whole computation small despite the exponential number of ways to parenthesize.

edge_inputs.description: a single operation, so no alternate grouping is possible at all
an all-multiplication expression, where every grouping yields the same product
single-digit operands at the maximum value with a mix of operators
many operators near the allowed maximum, mixing addition and multiplication throughout
an answer that happens to equal both the correct value and a wrong-order value must only ever score once, as correct
duplicate answers submitted by several students must each be scored independently

stress.description: random single-digit expressions near the maximum operator count, with random plausible answers
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    num_ops = max(1, min(size, 15))
    n = num_ops + 1
    if 2 * n - 1 > 31:
        n = 16
        num_ops = 15
    digits = [rng.randint(0, 9) for _ in range(n)]
    ops = [rng.choice('+*') for _ in range(num_ops)]
    parts = [str(digits[0])]
    for i in range(num_ops):
        parts.append(ops[i])
        parts.append(str(digits[i + 1]))
    s = ''.join(parts)
    num_answers = max(1, min(10000, size * 50))
    answers = [rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(num_answers)]
    return [s, answers]

stress.description: the maximum number of submitted answers against a fixed moderate expression
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    s = '1+2*3+4*5+6+7*8+9+1*2+3'
    num_answers = max(1, min(10000, size))
    answers = [rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(num_answers)]
    return [s, answers]

complexity.time: O(n^3) over the number of operators
